nativeRange.habitat: Seasonally flooded freshwater wetlands of Australia — floodplains, swamps, marshes, and the muddy margins of ponds where the species grows both emersed and submerged.

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich aquasoil or fine sand/gravel over root tabs
open foreground with direct overhead light for a tight carpet
gentle, steady flow over the rhizome line
optional pressurised CO2 for faster, denser carpeting
minimum substrate depth of roughly 2–5 cm to anchor runners

avoidWith: large boisterous fish that uproot foreground plants
vigorously burrowing loaches and large catfish

summary: Marsilea hirsuta is an Australian dwarf aquatic fern grown as a forgiving foreground carpet. Generally considered one of the easier alternatives to Dwarf Baby Tears, with clover-like leaves that shift between one and four leaflets depending on light and CO2.
careNotes: Marsilea hirsuta — sold under names like 'Hairy Water Clover', 'Australian Four-Leaf Clover', and 'Nardoo' — is an aquatic fern in family Marsileaceae (order Salviniales) native to seasonally flooded wetlands, floodplains, and swamps across Australia. It is the Australian sister to the Southeast Asian Marsilea crenata (fw-plant-037) and the temperate Marsilea quadrifolia, and like its relatives it shows strong phenotypic plasticity: emersed and freshly converted plants tend to grow the classic four-leaflet clover shape, while established submerged plants commonly produce stems with one to three (sometimes four) leaflets, with leaflet count and overall height shifting with light, CO2, and nutrients. Submerged height generally falls in the 2–10 cm range; under medium-to-high light the plant stays compact and creeps horizontally, while under low light it has been reported to grow taller, leggier, and more slowly. Sources consistently rate care as easy to beginner and notably less demanding than Hemianthus callitrichoides 'Cuba'. Reported water-parameter spread across sources is wide but compatible: temperature roughly 20–28 °C (with ~22–24 °C optimal; AquariumBreeder advises against sustained temperatures above ~26 °C), pH around 6.0–7.5, and soft to moderately hard water (gH ~2–15+). A nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) or an inert substrate supplemented with root tabs is generally advised — the plant is primarily a root feeder. Medium to high lighting (~30–60 PAR per AquariumBreeder) is preferred for a dense, tight carpet; the species tolerates low light but will trade carpeting form for taller, slower growth. CO2 is not required and the plant will carpet without it, but pressurised CO2 has been reported to noticeably speed up coverage and tighten leaflet spacing. Growth rate is generally slow to moderate; sources agree it is reliable rather than fast. Planted by tucking small rhizome clumps with their roots into the substrate every few centimetres; established mats can be trimmed and divided to expand coverage.
breedingNotes: Aquarium propagation is vegetative: the rhizome creeps horizontally as a runner/stolon and sends up new fronds at intervals, and aquascapers expand coverage by lifting and dividing established clumps or by cutting lateral shoots with attached roots and replanting them across the foreground. As with other Marsilea, sexual reproduction in the wild proceeds via sporocarps (the hardened bean-like spore cases characteristic of Marsileaceae), but sporocarp reproduction is essentially never used in submerged aquarium conditions.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica search page (the manifest-supplied primary URL) returned only navigation chrome via WebFetch with no plant-detail body content. The actual Tropica plant-detail page (https://tropica.com/en/plants/plantdetails/Marsileahirsuta(010TC)/4428) was fetched as a fallback and confirmed origin Australia, low light, low CO2 demand, medium growth rate, foreground carpeting placement, easy difficulty, and runner-based propagation.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked foreground carpet placement, clover-like 1–4 lobed morphology that varies with conditions, height 2–10 cm, medium-to-high light for compact growth, nutrient-rich substrate / root-tab requirement, easy care rating, and runner-based propagation. Source did not state CO2, temperature, or hardness numbers.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked Australian origin, family Marsileaceae, dwarf clover-like foreground placement, medium-to-high light for low growth (low light → taller), CO2 not required but recommended, pH range 6–7, slow/moderate growth rate, propagation by cut lateral shoots, and easy care rating.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (AquariumBreeder)
additional.notes: Cross-checked family Marsileaceae, Australian native range (marshes, ponds, floodplains), max submerged height ~10 cm, medium-to-high light (30–60 PAR), CO2 not required, slow-to-moderate growth, temperature 20–25 °C (cap ~26 °C), pH 6.0–7.5 (prefers 6.0–7.0), hardness gH 2–12, foreground-to-midground placement, easy-to-medium care rating, runner-based propagation, and nutrient-rich substrate requirement with minimum ~2.5–5 cm depth.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used only for taxonomy and range fallback: confirmed family Marsileaceae, order Salviniales, class Polypodiopsida, division Polypodiophyta, Australian endemism (floodplains and swamps), the common name 'Nardoo', and the original 1810 description by Robert Brown in Prodromus Florae Novae Hollandiae.
